title: Microsoft Graph - Defender for Identity And Defender for Office 365 API Patterns
applies_to: Extracting Defender for Identity sensor health and identity health issues
Querying Defender for Office 365 email threat data via Graph security alerts
Combining identity and email threat signals with endpoint data in security reports

confirmed_facts.fact: Defender for Identity data in Graph is under the `/security/identities` namespace. Two endpoints: health issues at `GET /security/identities/healthIssues` and sensors at `GET /security/identities/sensors`. Both require `IdentityRiskEvent.Read.All` or `SecurityIdentitiesHealth.Read.All` application permission and a Defender for Identity plan or Microsoft 365 E5/A5 license.

List Defender for Identity health issues: `GET /security/identities/healthIssues`. Returns a collection with `id`, `displayName`, `healthIssueType` (Sensor, DirectoryServices, etc.), `severity` (low, medium, high), `status` (open, closed), `domainNames`, `description`, and `recommendations`. Filter open high-severity issues: `$filter=status eq 'open' and severity eq 'high'`.

List Defender for Identity sensors: `GET /security/identities/sensors`. Returns each sensor's `id`, `displayName`, `sensorType` (AdConnectIntegrated, AdFsIntegrated, DomainControllerIntegrated, etc.), `status` (running, stopped, disconnected), `version`, `domainName`, and `healthStatus`. Use this to audit sensor coverage across domain controllers.

Defender for Office 365 alerts surface through the unified alerts endpoint `/security/alerts_v2`. Filter by service source: `GET /security/alerts_v2?$filter=serviceSource eq 'microsoftDefenderForOffice365'`. Alert categories include phishing, malware, suspicious email, BEC (business email compromise). Each alert has `evidence` array with affected mailboxes, users, and email metadata.

Other available `serviceSource` filter values for alerts_v2: `microsoftDefenderForEndpoint`, `microsoftDefenderForIdentity`, `microsoftDefenderForCloudApps`, `microsoftDefenderForOffice365`, `microsoft365Defender`, `azureActiveDirectory`, `microsoftSecurityExpertsDR`. Use these to segregate alerts by source in security dashboards.

Required permissions for Defender for Identity and Office 365 alert data: `SecurityAlert.Read.All` (application) for alerts_v2, `IdentityRiskEvent.Read.All` for identity-specific risk events, `SecurityIdentitiesHealth.Read.All` for health issues and sensors. All require admin consent.

implementation_notes: For a unified security dashboard, collect alerts from all service sources via a single `GET /security/alerts_v2` call with a date filter, then group by `serviceSource` in the reporting layer. This is more efficient than separate calls per product.

Defender for Identity sensor health is a useful operational metric to include in security reports: it shows whether MDI coverage is complete across all domain controllers. Include sensor count, running percentage, and open health issues in the executive summary.
Combine Defender for Identity health issues with Defender for Endpoint device data for a holistic identity + endpoint risk view in Power BI.
caveats: Defender for Identity APIs require an active Defender for Identity subscription. Tenants without MDI configured will receive empty results, not an error.
Defender for Office 365 alert evidence arrays can be large (many affected mailboxes). Use $select to limit fields and avoid context overload when storing to SharePoint.
Identity health issues and sensor endpoints are in v1.0 GA but availability in GCC High/DoD clouds should be verified before deployment in government tenants.
